data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0758 GMT - Kuaishou Technology's outlook looks positive in 2Q with steady core businesses and encouraging AI progress, according to Deutsche Bank Research analyst Leo Chiang in commentary. Kling AI, a tool for creating imaginative images and videos, continued to gain traction, generating 150 million yuan in revenue in the first quarter, Chiang says. This progress is likely to continue, he adds. The analyst expects its core ads and e-commerce businesses to maintain steady growth in 2Q as it continues to enhance its video-content ecosystem and monetization capabilities. DB keeps a buy rating, with a target price of HK$75.00. Shares were last traded at HK$53.00. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0400 GMT - AI could continue to drive long-term equity performance despite near-term volatility, UBS global wealth management chief investment office says in a research note. UBS expects global AI spending to reach $360 billion in 2025, up 60%, and further grow to $480 billion in 2026. Recent results from the four leading global cloud platforms showed a solid 24% on-year growth in combined revenue, they note, adding that robust cloud growth should narrow the gap between AI spending and monetization, which is also supported by rising AI adoption rates across industries and company sizes. Despite being bullish on the long-term trend, UBS favors a more balanced exposure between semiconductor and software names in the near term to navigate volatility. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0351 GMT - Superloop retains its status as Morgans analysts' pick of Australian telecom stocks, helped by an ungeared balance sheet that supports a range of options. Morgans analysts tell clients in a note that the stock is close to fair value based on current earnings, but say they see potential upside from strong sales momentum and the balance-sheet options. They note that telecom companies typically can handle net debt of one or two times Ebitda, which indicates Superloop could return capital to shareholders or undertake acquisitions, in which they see the latter as more likely. Morgans raises its target price by 15% to A$3.00 and keeps an add rating on the stock, which is down 1.1% at A$2.67. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1815 GMT - ATS' backlog is at a record high and should support revenue growth and some margin growth in fiscal 2026. TD Cowen's Cherilyn Radbourne says in a report that ATS has a backlog of C$2 billion, up 19% year over year. She notes that the backlog in the highly regulated life sciences market declined marginally quarter-on-quarter, but is up 38% year-over-year and remains by far the largest backlog contributor at 56%. ATS set new backlog records in consumer products, food & beverage, and nuclear, which reduced EVs to 10% from 12% in F3Q. Meanwhile, tariffs shouldn't be an issue either. ATS is monitoring tariff-related economic uncertainty, but hasn't seen material impact on bookings to-date, she adds. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1702 GMT - Mortgage rates rose to 6.89% this week, posing significant challenges for homebuyers and keeping the "lock-in effect" in place, says Realtor.com senior economist Jake Krimmel. According to Freddie Mac the 30-year mortgage rate rose 3 basis points to its highest level since Feb. 6. "With rates threatening to cross 7% for the first time since January," Krimmel says, "the 'lock-in effect'--where current homeowners are reluctant to sell and give up their lower mortgage rates--shows no signs of easing up." According to a recent Realtor.com survey, just 2% of respondents said they would be willing to buy with rates above 6%. But new listings and total homes for sale continued to rise last month, and homes are staying on the market for longer. Buyers may be regaining some bargaining power despite rising interest rates. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1451 GMT - Millennials are the only generation with a greater interest in buying a home in the next six months compared to last September, according to Realtor.com. Millennials' buying intention grew to 23% this year compared to last September's 15%, although most Americans--69%--aren't planning to engage in a real estate transaction in the next six months. High mortgage rates continue to impact the market. Younger generations are particularly affected, with over half of Millennials and Gen Z stating they've postponed their purchase. Gen Z appears to be the most cautious and wary of high mortgage rates, showing an increased inclination toward renting. A majority--57%--across all generations have used personal savings to finance their home purchase, while 15% have accessed personal investments or retirement accounts. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1441 GMT - Nvidia demonstrated in 1Q that it could continue to grow even with restrictions on chip sales to China, but Benchmark analyst Cody Acree believes the technology giant will still have to find a way to serve that enormous market. "The China market is too large and strategically important for the industry's leading solution supplier to be absent for long," Acree says. Acree expects Nvidia will offer a Chinese-tailored design variant, as he believes Chief Executive Jensen Huang will do whatever it takes to reclaim the lost opportunity. Although Acree notes that Huang "was noticeably coy" about Nvidia's limited options during the call. Nvidia is up 4%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1429 GMT - Nvidia's 1Q earnings helped quell concerns about how much the technology giant would suffer from the Trump administration's ban on chip sales to China, UBS analysts say. While 1Q results were generally in line with expectations, management's commentary on gross margins were better than investors were expecting, they say. Nvidia's results indicate 2Q revenue would have been $5 billion to $6 billion ahead of Wall Street expectations if it weren't for the limit on China sales, demonstrating strengthening underlying fundamentals, the analysts say. The rapid spread of AI across the world, and the sheer size of AI projects globally make the analysts confident Nvidia can still post strong growth despite the China ban. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1420 GMT - Salesforce's 1Q earnings weren't game changing, but they offered relief for investors that demand is still stable, Bank of America analysts Brad Sills and Carly Liu say. Strength in Salesforce's AI business drove higher-than-anticipated cRPO, or current remaining performance obligations, a key metric for how much revenue it expects to recognize from contracts. The fact that Salesforce reiterated its full-year outlook for subscription growth indicates demand is staying stable, despite concerns that weaker channel tone would prompt a guide down, the analysts say. Salesforce also upped its revenue guidance after a 1Q beat, and Sills and Liu believe AI's growth will continue to help sales outperform. Salesforce sinks 7%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1005 GMT - Distilled artificial intelligence models could lead to a new era of portable AI servers, Citi analysts say in a research note. Recent AI distillation technology, pioneered by DeepSeek, has enabled a significant reduction in AI models' sizes, they say. This is likely to support portable AI servers, accelerate on-device AI demand and AI semiconductor content growth, they say. Citi expects AI servers to be "extremely efficient and so compact that one day people will carry them in their hands," in forms of smartphones, laptops, smart glasses and robots. Unlike AI servers used in data centers, portable AI servers would likely require low power consumption and better thermal efficiency, they add. Citi names SK Hynix, Samsung, TSMC and others to be the top beneficiaries of this trend. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0924 GMT - Nvidia's sales projections for the current quarter bode well for European semiconductor companies with exposure to artificial intelligence like ASML Holding, ASM International, Infineon Technologies and Arm Holdings, Citi analysts say. Nvidia is forecasting revenue of $45 billion, plus or minus 2%, this quarter even as it expects to lose about $8 billion in sales of H20 chips to China after the Trump administration imposed restrictions. "We think increased conviction around future AI demand is supportive for European semiconductor names too," the analysts write in a note to clients. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0858 GMT - Nvidia keeps growing at an impressive rate despite all the challenges the trade war throws its way, Swissquote Bank's Ipek Ozkardeskaya writes in a note to clients. "We don't know how it does it, but it does it. Nvidia continues to defy gravity," Ozkardeskaya says. Revenue for Nvidia's fiscal first quarter that closed April 27 jumped 69% on year to $44.06 billion, above a FactSet estimate of about $43.10 billion. Ozkardeskaya notes the company logged that growth rate even after taking a $4.5 billion charge linked with excess inventory and purchase obligations for H20 chips that Nvidia couldn't sell to China because of U.S. restrictions. Nvidia shares are up 5.5% premarket at $142.26. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0632 GMT - News that Swiss eyecare specialist Alcon received its first U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval since its listing in 2019 is slightly positive, but comes as little surprise, Vontobel's Sibylle Bischofberger says in a research note. The drug, Tryptyr, was approved to treat the signs and symptoms of dry eye disease, a condition that affects around 38 million people in the U.S. and for which there is a need for new treatment options, the analyst says. "We expect Alcon to continue performing well, especially with the launch of several products in 2025. However, results may remain somewhat volatile due to challenging market conditions," Vontobel says. Zurich-listed shares closed 1.95% lower Wednesday at 70.56 Swiss francs, with markets in Switzerland closed Thursday due to a holiday. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0535 GMT - Bangkok Dusit Medical Services' earnings outlook appears softer, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. Management has guided for 2025 revenue growth of 4%-6%, down from its earlier 7%-8% growth target for 1H 2025, which probably reflects a weaker economic outlook for Thailand, the analyst says. Management also noted Middle East revenue may decline in the long term as countries in the region invest in their own healthcare systems, the analyst says. The brokerage reduces its 2025-2027 earnings estimates for the Thai hospital operator by 2%-5%. It lowers the stock's target price to THB27.50 from THB31.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are unchanged at THB21.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0317 GMT - IHH Healthcare is poised for long-term growth, backed by solid execution, a strong regional presence, and resilient demand for premium healthcare, says RHB IB analyst Oong Chun Sung in a note. He is upbeat on IHH's organic and inorganic growth strategies, especially after Malaysia scrapped plans for a diagnosis-related group payment system, which removes a key regulatory overhang. DRG is a system that groups hospital cases by diagnosis and treatment, with each group reimbursed at a fixed rate regardless of actual treatment cost. Oong raises IHH 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 7%, 11% and 11%, respectively, after 1Q earnings beat expectation. RHB raises IHH's target price to MYR9.00 from MYR8.70, while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.7% lower at MYR6.86.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0218 GMT - Bangkok Chain Hospital's tone seems positive on the recent high budget spending by Thailand's Social Security Office, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report, as they maintain a buy rating on the stock. Management clarified that this spending won't affect the current "AdjRW>2" rate of THB12,000 per patient, easing investors' concerns over the past few days, the analysts say. SSO's payments to flood victims in September 2024 and earthquake victims in March 2025 raised concerns that the SSO may lack funds to maintain the current rate, they note. However, the brokerage lowers the stock's target price to THB17.50 from THB20.00, based on its 2025 enterprise value/Ebitda multiple of 14.0x. Shares last closed at THB13.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0139 GMT - Aroa Biosurgery's conservative revenue guidance implies that the wound-treatment company's elevated growth momentum should continue, according to Wilsons analysts. They write in a note that the guidance is seen as achievable by the market, adding that they anticipate a potentially significant increase in productivity from its Myriad product sales reps in fiscal 2026. They reckon that the stock should trade in line with wound-care peers at three times 2026 revenue. Wilsons lifts its target price 9.3% to A$0.82 and stays overweight on the stock, which is flat at A$0.53. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0105 GMT - IHH Healthcare is poised for continued growth, supported by a planned 28% increase in hospital bed capacity by 2028, mainly in Malaysia and India, Hong Leong IB analyst Chee Kok Siang says in a note. In Singapore and Hong Kong, IHH plans to expand across the care continuum with a focus on ambulatory and primary care, while also aiming to turn around its China operations, he says. Ongoing refurbishments at Singapore's Mount Elizabeth Hospital at Orchard may pressure margins until 2Q, he adds. Management remains open to merger-and-acquisition opportunities, targeting value-accretive assets in Malaysia, India, Turkey, and Europe, he notes. Hong Leong raises IHH's target price to MYR9.06 from MYR9.03 while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are 0.7% lower at MYR6.86.(yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0050 GMT - If Ryman Healthcare has a vision for the next three to five years that goes beyond modest improvements in cash generation then management doesn't appear ready to share it with the market, says Forsyth Barr. It had hoped Ryman's FY 2025 result would offer clarity on the path ahead, stating it's "a raison d'etre to own Ryman through what is undoubtedly going to be a prolonged turnaround." Instead, Ryman's FY 2026 guidance disappointed. Ryman expects 1,200 total sales at the midpoint of its guidance range, substantially below Forsyth Barr's estimate of 1,350 ahead of its result. "The lower sales expectation is likely the core driver of Ryman's unwillingness to guide to positive free cash flow for FY 2026--another disappointment," analyst Aaron Ibbotson says. Forsyth Barr retains a neutral call on Ryman. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0712 GMT - M&G's partnership with Japanese life insurance company Dai-ichi Life is very positive news for the U.K. investment management firm and will boost its shares, J.P. Morgan analysts say. The deal is a large strategic commitment from Japan's largest listed life insurer, they note. Dai-ichi Life's decision to acquire a significant stake in M&G shows a high level of confidence in the execution of the London-listed company's strategy. The two partners could also develop new products together, the analysts add. Shares in M&G are up 8% at 2.42 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0044 GMT - A shift in deposit mix looks like being a stronger margin headwind for Westpac than for National Australia Bank, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They point to Westpac's larger exposure to retail banking and the pricing of its retail savings accounts as justification for this view. The MS analysts observe in a note to clients that the proportion of savings in term deposits remains lower than at the end of the last decade, and that customers are still shifting toward higher-cost deposits such as those offering bonus enticements. This is one of the reasons that MS prefers NAB over Westpac among Australian bank stocks. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1702 GMT - Mortgage rates rose to 6.89% this week, posing significant challenges for homebuyers and keeping the "lock-in effect" in place, says Realtor.com senior economist Jake Krimmel. According to Freddie Mac the 30-year mortgage rate rose 3 basis points to its highest level since Feb. 6. "With rates threatening to cross 7% for the first time since January," Krimmel says, "the 'lock-in effect'--where current homeowners are reluctant to sell and give up their lower mortgage rates--shows no signs of easing up." According to a recent Realtor.com survey, just 2% of respondents said they would be willing to buy with rates above 6%. But new listings and total homes for sale continued to rise last month, and homes are staying on the market for longer. Buyers may be regaining some bargaining power despite rising interest rates. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1451 GMT - Millennials are the only generation with a greater interest in buying a home in the next six months compared to last September, according to Realtor.com. Millennials' buying intention grew to 23% this year compared to last September's 15%, although most Americans--69%--aren't planning to engage in a real estate transaction in the next six months. High mortgage rates continue to impact the market. Younger generations are particularly affected, with over half of Millennials and Gen Z stating they've postponed their purchase. Gen Z appears to be the most cautious and wary of high mortgage rates, showing an increased inclination toward renting. A majority--57%--across all generations have used personal savings to finance their home purchase, while 15% have accessed personal investments or retirement accounts. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1419 GMT - Southwest Airlines is set to take off in the eyes of Deutsche Bank. Analysts upgrade the Dallas airline to buy from hold for three reasons. They see Southwest in the middle of its largest transformation and are confident in a successful outcome thanks to the new board, ushered in part by activist investor Elliott Investment Management's stake in the company, and management team. The analysts also believe Southwest's current strategic initiatives, including bag fees and the launch of a basic economy product, will drive growth over the next one to two years, and its return on invested capital to significantly improve over the next two years. They raise their 2025, 2026 and 2027 EPS estimates. Shares rise 2.6%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0228 GMT - Tenaga Nasional is set to benefit from record electricity demand driven by data centers, commercial growth and hot weather, Affin Hwang IB analyst Isaac Chow says in a note. He expects electricity sales to rebound strongly in 2Q amid rising peak demand. Tenaga has completed 21 data-center projects, and 17 are under construction, he notes. Management also sees continued strong interest from data-center developers and aims to secure an additional 10 electricity supply agreements, each with a maximum demand of 150 MW to 200 MW by end-2025, he adds. Chow cuts Tenaga's 2025-2027 earnings forecasts by 4%-8% amid a higher tax rate. Affin Hwang lowers the target price to MYR16.20 from MYR16.70, with a buy rating. Shares fall 1.0% to MYR13.94. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0134 GMT - China Aviation Oil (Singapore) may benefit from some tailwinds, Phillip Securities Research's Liu Miaomiao says in a report. Profit from its associates is expected to grow at least 12% in 2025, supported by continued rebound of inbound leisure and business travel, the analyst says. Also, the company's volume in trading of jet fuel and other oil products is likely to pick up in 1H, driven by rising demand for jet fuel and sustainable aviation fuel, the analyst says. The brokerage upgrades the stock's rating to accumulate from neutral and raises the target price to S$0.90 from S$0.85 to reflect recent share-price performance and improving financial metrics. Shares last closed at S$0.83. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2248 GMT - Forsyth Barr worries Infratil's operating cash flow could worsen if it sells certain assets to realize some NZ$1 billion of proceeds. Infratil signaled the goal this week, stating that businesses unlikely to scale under its ownership are candidates for sale. Analyst Aaron Ibbotson thinks Diagnostic Imaging, Wellington International Airport, a 10% stake in Contact Energy, and RetireAustralia fit that description. "However, outside of RA, those three businesses provide the lion's share of operating cash flow," Forsyth Barr says. Infratil suggests its CDC data centers and Longroad Energy businesses could pick up the tab, but Forsyth Barr says that is many years away. "We applaud the clarified focus on having a sustainable operating-cash-flow position, but short of a dividend reduction we do not see a clear path," the bank says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1912 GMT - The boost to oil from a U.S. court ruling against President Trump's trade tariffs proves short-lived, and futures turn lower with OPEC+ supply concerns remaining as a headwind. Another 411,000-barrel increase in July would bring accumulated unwinding of output cuts to 1.37 million b/d, although "the actual supply boost may be significantly less than projected," StoneX's Alex Hodes says in a note. U.S. inventory data, including a 2.8 million barrel crude stock draw, largely on higher exports, did little for prices. Gasoline stocks fell more than expected as demand picked up to almost 9.5 million b/d ahead of the summer driving season. WTI settles down 1.5% at $60.94 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.2% to $64.15. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1547 GMT - Oil futures fall further after relinquishing gains from a federal court ruling against President Trump's tariffs. Oil has been drifting lower on expectations that OPEC+ will agree to add another 411,000 barrels a day to July production targets. The increases are likely aimed at maintaining flexibility under favorable market conditions--global crude inventories were near 4-year lows for much of 1Q--and at defending market share, Kenny Zhu of Global X says in a note. "The decision to raise output is likely to continue adding downward pressure to crude oil prices over the intermediate term," he says, although "oil demand has held up relatively well compared to expectations, and may help absorb some of the increased output even as higher cost non-OPEC producers reduce production." WTI is down 1.2% at $61.08 a barrel, and Brent is off 1.1% at $64.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1305 GMT - Oil futures turn lower after spiking overnight on a U.S. federal court's ruling against President Trump's tariffs, which the administration says it intends to appeal. The market appears still focused on the OPEC+ meeting this weekend where the group is expected to unwind another 411,000 barrels a day of production cuts in July. The API's report of a 4.2 million barrel drawdown last week in U.S. crude oil stocks is "unlikely to be replicated" in the EIA data, Ritterbusch says in a note, although another small draw at Cushing, the Nymex delivery hub, "will help keep the WTI curve supported." WTI is down 0.4% at $61.58 a barrel, and Brent is 0.3% lower at $64.71.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0718 GMT - Bajaj Auto's business focus is likely to offer sufficient operational levers to sustain margins despite rising input costs, Axis Securities' Shridhar Kallani says in a research report. This focus includes expansion of its portfolio of premium motorcycles, electric two-wheelers and electric three-wheelers, and leveraging of its new product launches, the analyst notes. For FY 2026, management is seeking to gain leadership in the 125cc+ motorcycle segment, scale key two- and three-wheeler platforms and increase exports, particularly in Latin America, the analyst adds. The brokerage raises the stock's target price to INR9,890.00 from INR9,380.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 2.5% lower at INR8,652.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0647 GMT - SIA Engineering stands to benefit from new comprehensive services agreements signed with Singapore Airlines and Scoot recently, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a research report. Based on the maintenance, repair, and overhaul services provider's announcement on the Singapore Exchange, the agreements are expected to generate a total labor revenue of S$1.3 billion over two years. This translates to a significant labor revenue step-up of 54.7% per annum when compared to the previous agreements, the analyst estimates. OCBC updates its model to incorporate the new contracts, and raises the stock's fair value estimate to S$3.00 from S$2.64 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 0.4% lower at S$2.71. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0607 GMT - Samvardhana Motherson International may benefit from its entry into new segments, Nomura analysts say in a research report, as they raise the stock's target price to INR170.00 from INR155.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Management has commented that the automotive component supplier will target new solutions like components for semiconductor machines and printed circuit board assemblies for autos, the analysts note. Management also said the Indian company will seek to continue ramping up production of consumer electronics. The brokerage raises its FY 2026-2027 revenue estimates for the company by 3%-5% to reflect a stronger EUR/INR, partly offset by lower organic growth assumptions. Shares are 1.7% higher at INR154.75. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0509 GMT - Somboon Advance Technology faces an earnings hit from Thailand's weak economy, which is likely to continue weighing on the auto industry, Thanachart Securities' Rata Limsuthiwanpoom says in a research report. Thailand's auto production fell 12% on year during first four months of 2025, with the auto sector likely to stay under pressure from a global slowdown driven by U.S. tariffs, the analyst says. The brokerage cuts its 2025-2027 earnings forecasts for the Thai auto-parts manufacturer by 21%-40%, and lowers the stock's target price to THB14.00 from THB16.00. However, the brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating on the valuation and dividend yield angles. Shares are 1.6% higher at THB12.40. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0043 GMT - Mainfreight's earnings cycle appears to have troughed to Forsyth Barr. The logistics group's FY 2025 was mixed with a strong performance in Australia more than offset by profit declines in New Zealand, the Americas and Asia. Analyst Andy Bowley sees scope for Mainfreight's earnings to accelerate over the next 18 months, returning to trend growth in FY 2027. Still, Forsyth Barr warns this recovery is likely to be non-linear. "Uncertainty persists, particularly around tariffs, with a volume slowdown through May offset by strong forward bookings for June/July and accompanied by volatility in rates," Forsyth Barr says. It notes that Mainfreight is reluctant to talk about "green shoots" in New Zealand just yet. The bank retains a neutral call on Mainfreight. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1630 GMT - United Airlines has no plans to acquire JetBlue Airways, Chief Executive Scott Kirby said Thursday during a Wall Street Journal Future of Everything interview. He said his experience overseeing mergers during his career have been challenging and require a lot of work to integrate technology and cultures. He prefers the kind of deal like the one United announced Thursday, in which JetBlue and United will allow each other to sell seats on the other's sites. That allows United to sell seats at New York's JFK airport, which Kirby said will be a win for its frequent-fliers. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1624 GMT - United Airlines took a hit to bookings after problems at its Newark Airport hub resulted in capacity cuts and fewer staffers. Chief Executive Scott Kirby said load factors at Newark dropped 15 points in April, but have started to recover, and the airline is using aircraft with more seats to offset the fact that it has to operate fewer planes. He believes ticket prices at Newark will be particularly low for the summer because of the drop in demand. Kirby expects the decline to be felt in United's earnings this quarter, and said he anticipates earnings would have come in around the upper edge of its guidance range, but will instead be in the middle. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1621 GMT - United Airlines Chief Executive Scott Kirby is extremely optimistic about the government's role in overhauling airline safety and regulation. He said during a Wall Street Journal Future of Everything interview Thursday that the Federal Aviation Administration is limiting the number of flights that touch down at Newark Airport after years of Kirby asking the organization to do so. He believes that change will fix the majority of the delay and safety issues at the airport. Still, the FAA needs more staffing, he said, which is largely a problem stemming from low capacity at the agency's training program. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1429 GMT - BRP CEO Jose Boisjoli expects margins to improve in the back half of the year as inventories improve and margins get a boost. The executive says that 3Q margins are expected to be around flat, but to accelerate in the 4Q as new products are launched and the inventory levels begin to normalize. The company's inventory levels had been a thorn in the company's side for a number of quarters as its dealers struggled to move products and therefore were forced to resort to more promotions. TD Cowen's Brian Morrison noted that BRP dealer inventory is down 21% year-over-year, "reaching their target range, aided by the late-season, weather-induced lift in snowmobile sales." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1419 GMT - Southwest Airlines is set to take off in the eyes of Deutsche Bank. Analysts upgrade the Dallas airline to buy from hold for three reasons. They see Southwest in the middle of its largest transformation and are confident in a successful outcome thanks to the new board, ushered in part by activist investor Elliott Investment Management's stake in the company, and management team. The analysts also believe Southwest's current strategic initiatives, including bag fees and the launch of a basic economy product, will drive growth over the next one to two years, and its return on invested capital to significantly improve over the next two years. They raise their 2025, 2026 and 2027 EPS estimates. Shares rise 2.6%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1328 GMT - BRP's CFO Sebastien Martel says on an analyst call that "so far, the impact of tariffs remains manageable, since all our vehicles produced in Canada and Mexico are USMCA compliant." The company isn't invulnerable, however. "We've seen incremental tariffs on other countries, primarily affecting our P&A business, suppliers and indirectly us," he says. BRP sees for the year a tariff hit of C$60 million to C$70 million. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1258 GMT - BRP is still coping with slowing demand, and it's weighing on revenue. In a Stifel report, analyst Martin Landry notes that 1Q revenue fell but came in better than expected. Still, this quarter's downturn marks the seventh consecutive quarter of year-over-year sales decline as the powersports industry "copes with a slowdown in demand," Landry says. If BRP's revenues were to show a sign of stabilization, Landry says that this would help investor sentiment and could even suggest that inventories are better aligned with demand. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: CI Com to Seek More Time for FY24 Annual Report Publication
data.description:
CI Com plans to seek another extension from the SIX Swiss Exchange to publish its 2024 annual report after failing to appoint an auditor at its extraordinary general meeting on Wednesday.

The initial publication deadline was set for Saturday, according to a Friday release. The proposed auditors declined their election after Sebrina Holding Suisse questioned the acquisition of a majority stake in the Swiss-listed investment holding company.

As other directors also refused their election during the meeting, Chairman Michel Rethoret reversed his planned resignation and was re-elected alongside two new directors to avoid a leadership vacuum.

data.title: Sichuan Huiyu Pharma Gets Nod to Market Cancer Drug in UK
data.description:
Sichuan Huiyu Pharmaceutical's unit, Seacross Pharmaceuticals, received marketing authorization from the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency for cyclophosphamide for injection, according to a Shanghai bourse disclosure on Friday.

The drug is used to treat various cancers, such as leukemia, lymphoma, breast cancer and lung cancer.

The Chinese pharmaceutical company is allowed to market the drug in other European countries, such as Ireland, Portugal, France, the Netherlands, Italy and Spain.

Meanwhile, the drug is still waiting for approval from the authorities in Germany.

The company's shares closed over 4% higher.

data.title: Renewables supply to fall on Monday while demand rises
data.description:
German and French spot prices were not yet traded early on Friday, while lower renewables output and an increase in demand are set to drive Monday prices higher.

The German and French Monday baseload power contracts were both untraded by 0737 GMT, LSEG data showed. (TRDEBD3), (TRFRBD3)

Residual load is seen increasing on Monday throughout the region, driven by a drop in wind and solar supply as well as higher consumption, LSEG analyst Naser Hashemi said.

German wind power output was expected to fall sharply by 12.2 GW to 9.8 GW on Monday, while French wind supply was seen up 490 MW to 1.7 GW, LSEG data showed.

German solar power supply was projected to shed 660 MW to 14.7 GW, while the French output was seen down 1.6 GW to 3.8 GW LSEG data showed.

Power consumption in Germany is forecast to rise 1.8 GW to 51.1 GW on Monday, while demand in France is seen up 2.5 GW to 40.2 GW, the data showed.

French nuclear availability rose two percentage points to 66% of total capacity.

German year-ahead power (TRDEBYZ6) edged down 0.1% to 87.05 euros per megawatt hour (MWh), while the French 2025 baseload contract (TRFRBYZ6) closed with a bid/ask range between 60 euros/MWh and 61 euros/MWh on Thursday.

In the European carbon market (CFI2Zc1), the benchmark contract rose 0.4% to 71.21 euros a metric ton.

data.title: NBCC (India) Clocks Gains in Fiscal Q4 Consolidated Profit; Shares Up 5%
data.description:
NBCC (India) recorded a rise in its consolidated profit attributable to the owners to 1.76 billion Indian rupees in the fiscal fourth quarter ended March 31 from 1.36 billion rupees a year ago.

Earnings per share came in at 0.65 rupees compared with the year-ago figure of 0.50 rupees, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges by the construction and infrastructure company.

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q4 also increased to 46.4 billion rupees from 40.0 billion rupees a year earlier.

The company's board recommended a final dividend of 0.14 rupees per paid-up equity share of 1 rupee each for the financial year 2024-25.

NBCC's shares were up nearly 5% in recent trade.

data.title: Lingnan Group Says Guangzhou Duty-Free JV Completes Registration
data.description:
Guangzhou Lingnan Group said the registration for its CDF Downtown Duty-Free (Guangzhou) joint venture has been completed, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

CDF Downtown Duty-Free (Guangzhou) has a registered capital of 45 million yuan.

Guangzhou Lingnan Group holds a 19.5% stake in the joint venture. Other stakeholders include Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport at 10%, Guangzhou Grandbuy at 19.5% and China Duty Free Group at 51%.

data.title: Hang Seng Wraps Up Strong Month Despite Friday Slump
data.description:
The Hang Seng slipped 284 points or 1.2% to finish at 23,290 on Friday, pulling back from a strong session the day before.

The slump followed a U.S. federal court's decision to temporarily reinstate tariffs imposed by President Trump, reversing an earlier trade court ruling that had blocked them.

Most sectors fell, led by sharp losses in tech and consumer stocks.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessent remarked that trade talks with China were “a bit stalled” and may require direct intervention from Trump and Xi.

Meanwhile, China’s official May PMI, due over the weekend, kept markets cautious amid concerns over fragile factory activity.

Auto stocks extended losses on continued price war fears, with BYD down 3.6%.

Other decliners included Orient Overseas (-6.9%) and Techtronic Inds. (-4.5%).

Still, the index gained 5.3% for the month, buoyed by strong IPO momentum in Hong Kong.

Over 150 companies reportedly are in the pipeline to list in the city, with many targeting IPOs above $1 billion.

data.title: Italian GDP Growth Confirmed at 0.3%
data.description:
The Italian gross domestic product expanded by 0.3% in the first quarter of 2025, accelerating from the 0.1% growth rate in the previous period and in line with the first estimate, thus remaining above the initial market expectations of a 0.2% growth rate.

It was the sharpest pace of quarterly growth in one year, reflecting some traction for the Italian economy as lower interest rates and improved confidence on its traditionally risky debt, in relative terms, spurred investments.

Gross fixed investment jumped by 1.4% with support from buildings and structures (1.6%) and machinery (0.6%).

In turn, household consumption rose 0.6% and public expenditure jumped 0.7%.

Still, net external demand contributed negatively to GDP growth as imports surged 4.3% and exports added a softer 0.8%.

data.title: Dollar set for fifth monthly drop on trade, fiscal uncertainty
data.description:
Key points:
  • Upbeat Tokyo core inflation underpins yen
  • Fed's preferred PCE inflation data awaited
  • Dollar index set for fifth-straight monthly decline

The U.S. dollar wobbled on Friday en route to its fifth-straight monthly decline as traders braced for further uncertainty around trade policy and U.S. fiscal health, while awaiting pivotal inflation reports later in the day.

The greenback had a choppy week, ending lower in the previous session after a federal court temporarily reinstated the most sweeping of President Donald Trump's tariffs, just a day after another court had ordered an immediate block on them.

Trump said on Thursday he hoped the Supreme Court would overturn the trade court's decision, while officials also indicated that they could employ other presidential powers to ensure the tariffs take effect.

The uncertainty around tariffs has exerted a vice-like grip on markets as investors flee U.S. assets in a search for alternatives, worried that Trump's erratic policies could challenge the strength and outperformance of U.S. markets.

"The news is making the U.S. a less attractive place for foreign investors to go," said Kit Juckes, chief FX strategist at Societe Generale.

This will not stop money from flowing there, Juckes said, but investors will be looking for more attractive incentives such as a slightly weaker currency or high yields.

On Friday, the euro was slightly lower at $1.1331, ahead of German inflation data for May while the Swiss franc was little changed at 0.8243 per dollar.

The U.S. currency, though, was set for monthly declines against the Swiss franc, the euro and the pound .

Thursday's weekly jobless claims and economic growth data did little to placate worries of a U.S. economic downturn. Investor focus will be on the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation data - the personal consumption expenditure (PCE) report - later on Friday.

Worries about fiscal debt levels in developed economies, highlighted by weak appetite for freshly issued, longer-dated credit in the U.S. and in Japan, have also weighed.

The dollar index , which tracks the U.S. unit against a basket of six other currencies, was 0.3% higher at 99.56. The index was set for a decline of 0.10% in May, its fifth straight month in the red.

On the flip side, markets have taken note of emerging market assets. An index tracking emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) has gained about 2% for the month - its biggest one-month rise since November 2023.

INFLATION WATCH

The Japanese yen was little changed at 144.05 per dollar after data showed underlying inflation in Tokyo hit a more than two-year high in May, keeping alive the chances of further interest rate hikes from the Bank of Japan.

"The BOJ is in a difficult position," said Min Joo Kang, senior economist at ING. "Inflationary pressures remain hot, while the economic recovery remains fragile - and facing strong headwinds from U.S. tariffs."

However, the yen is on track for its first monthly decline against the dollar this year.

Markets are also on the lookout for clues on highly anticipated trade deals as the Trump-mandated July 9 deadline for implementation of tariffs draws near.

The U.S. PCE data is likely to show that inflation rose 2.2% in April, according to economists polled by Reuters, compared with a 2.3% increase in March.

The Fed tracks the PCE price measures for its 2% inflation target. Economists are forecasting a surge in inflation this year as the Trump administration's import duties raise the cost of goods.

"The danger is the next piece of news on, for example, the tariff impact on import prices", which however would come later in the year, Juckes said.

Elsewhere, the Australian dollar slid to $0.6421, while the New Zealand dollar was last bought at $0.5961.

data.title: Czech economy accelerates in first quarter on higher household spending
data.description:
Czech economic output jumped by a faster-than-predicted 0.8% in the first quarter, the highest since late 2021, driven by a strong rebound in household spending and inventories, the Czech Statistical Bureau (CSU) said on Friday.

The result marked an increase from a flash estimate of 0.5% expansion, and put year-on-year growth at 2.2%.

The central European economy has been slow to recover from the COVID pandemic, which was followed by an inflation wave, high energy prices and weak demand in main trade partner Germany.

A gradual rebound in real wages, as inflation waned, has been the main driver of the recovery.

The CSU said household demand contributed 1.5 percentage points to the year-on-year expansion, followed by inventory adding 1.3 points, government spending with 0.4, while foreign trade and capital investments had negative contributions.

"The year-on-year result is the best in nearly three years and was caused mainly by growing household spending," said Petr Dufek, chief economist at Banka Creditas.

"The first quarter result lays the ground quite well for the full-year growth of roughly 2%."

Chief Economist Pavel Sobisek of UniCredit CZ+SK said momentum would help the economy going forward but there would be negative effects of U.S. tariff policies.

"That will undoubtedly cause fluctuations of growth and its overall reduction versus a situation of tariffs not being imposed," he said in a note.

The crown was trading flat versus the euro at 24.945 .
